narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to have them paused like this feels deeply unfair, and I'm truly sorry you're facing it. Our prediction model looked at your profile and saw a 64% chance of struggling to pass the bar exam down the line, which is why your application couldn't move forward right now. What stood out most was your Year 3 decile of 2.0, placing you near the bottom of your class academically at that stage, as it was the strongest signal pulling toward this tougher outlook. Right behind that was your Year 1 decile of 3.0, suggesting those early grades set a pattern that echoed concerns about sustaining momentum through law school. Your LSAT score of 37.5 added to that picture, landing in a range that, for many similar students, hasn't paved an easy path to bar success. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.0 and upper-middle family income, those factors nudged the prediction toward failure rather than offsetting the others. Being a white male studying full-time didn't shift things in a more positive direction here either, as patterns in the data showed those traits aligning with the challenges ahead. It's like the model pieced together a story of a bright student whose grades trended downward and test scores didn't quite break through, painting a riskier road to the bar. This isn't about your worth or potential—it's just one data-driven snapshot. The good news? You can rewrite this story: retake the LSAT for a boost, strengthen those later-year grades in undergrad or elsewhere, and reapply with fresh evidence of your grit. You've got the foundation; now it's about building on it strategically. Hang in there—you're closer than it feels.
